skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python snippets through the inference.sh CLI with common data, web, media, document, and 3D libraries available in a remote sandbox.
skill.summary: Execute Python code with common libraries through an inference.sh sandbox.
skill.author: skillssh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution matches, but most are Markdown code examples and library names rather than hidden shell execution. The real risk is intentional: this community skill grants Bash access to infsh and sends arbitrary Python to an inference.sh sandbox, with documented network-capable libraries and API examples. No prompt injection, credential theft, weak cryptography, or malicious exfiltration intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Python Execution Through External CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly allows Bash(infsh *) and instructs users to submit arbitrary Python code to infsh/python-executor. This is the intended function, but it gives the assistant a broad code execution path through a third-party sandbox and should be treated as elevated risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and examples directly show infsh commands that execute user supplied Python code. The behavior is intentional and documented, not an inferred scanner artifact.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Python Workloads
medium_findings.description: The skill advertises HTTP clients, browser automation, web scraping, and API call examples. This is legitimate for the stated use case, but arbitrary Python with network libraries can access external services or expose sensitive data if prompts include secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation lists network libraries and includes requests.get examples to external URLs. I did not find evidence of malicious endpoints or automatic credential exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Matches Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but the reviewed occurrences are Markdown inline code, fenced shell examples, package names, or JSON examples. They are not executable code inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is a Markdown instruction file and the cited lines are formatted documentation. The confirmed executable surface is the allowed infsh Bash permission, which is tracked separately.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Lacks Evidence
low_findings.description: The high static finding for weak cryptography at line 3 appears to be a false positive. Line 3 is descriptive frontmatter and contains no hash, cipher, crypto API, or password handling logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line was inspected directly and contains the skill description with an inference.sh link. No cryptographic operation is present there.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Finding Lacks Evidence
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance finding at line 163 appears to be a false positive. The line says the executor runs in an isolated subprocess and does not describe scanning hosts, ports, or networks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line was inspected directly. It documents sandbox execution and does not contain reconnaissance tooling or behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad infsh Bash Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed-tools setting permits Bash commands matching infsh *. This is necessary for the skill, but marketplace users should understand that it delegates execution to an external CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission is declared in frontmatter and is broad within the infsh command namespace. Its practical risk depends on inference.sh account permissions and sandbox controls.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Code Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples pass a code field directly to the python-executor app. This is not hidden behavior, but it means prompt content can become executable Python in the sandbox.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples show Python source supplied through the code input field. The pattern is core to the skill and clearly documented.

content.user_title: Run Python Code in a Remote Sandbox
content.value_statement: Agents often need Python for analysis, automation, and file generation but may not have a local runtime. This skill routes Python jobs through inference.sh with common libraries already installed.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python code through the infsh/python-executor app using the inference.sh CLI.
Uses common libraries for HTTP, scraping, data analysis, plotting, media, PDFs, and 3D files.
Supports configurable timeouts from 1 to 300 seconds.
Can return files saved under the outputs directory.
Offers a high memory variant with 16GB RAM for larger jobs.
Provides examples for API calls, charts, images, video, and mesh generation.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated infsh session.
Runs in a CPU-only environment without GPU or dedicated machine learning libraries.
Uses a non-interactive runtime, so visual output must be saved to files.
Network requests depend on the remote sandbox and should not include secrets unless approved.

use_cases.title: Analyze a Small Dataset
use_cases.description: Run pandas transformations and save charts or CSV files when the local environment is unavailable.

use_cases.title: Automate Web Data Collection
use_cases.description: Use requests, BeautifulSoup, Selenium, or Playwright to collect public web data for routine workflows.

use_cases.title: Generate Media and Document Assets
use_cases.description: Create images, videos, PDFs, or 3D files from scripted Python tasks in a reproducible sandbox.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Python Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints the Python version and installed pandas version.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Chart from Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to load this small table, summarize it with pandas, and save a bar chart to outputs/chart.png.

prompt_templates.title: Scrape a Public Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch this public URL, extract the page title and main headings, and summarize the results.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Reproducible Asset Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to generate a CSV, a PNG visualization, and a PDF report from this dataset. Save all artifacts under outputs/.

output_examples.input: Run a pandas summary on a small sales table.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns row counts, totals, averages, and a saved chart artifact for download.

output_examples.input: Create a simple grayscale image using Pillow and NumPy.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports that the image was generated and returns the saved PNG from the outputs directory.

output_examples.input: Fetch a public API endpoint and summarize selected fields.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a concise summary of the response and notes any request or parsing errors.

best_practices: Review generated Python before execution when prompts include external input.
Save charts, reports, and media files under outputs so the skill can return them.
Use short timeouts first, then increase timeout or memory only when the task needs it.
anti_patterns: Do not send API keys, tokens, cookies, or private data unless the execution path is approved.
Do not use this skill for interactive notebooks, GUI windows, or commands that require live input.
Do not run untrusted scraping or automation against systems without permission.

faq.question: Does this run Python locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to run code in a remote sandbox.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented environment includes HTTP clients and web automation libraries.

faq.question: Can it return generated files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files saved under outputs are automatically returned by the executor.

faq.question: Is it safe for secrets?
faq.answer: Treat it as a remote execution service. Do not include secrets unless your organization approves that workflow.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate use, but it enables arbitrary Python execution and network-capable workloads through an external CLI.
